experiment-maya-test-1.name: Experiment: Maya Test 1 — Paper Due at Midnight

turns.content: I am Maya, I am a sophomore with ADHD working two jobs and I have a paper due tomorrow that I have not started. I do not even understand what the professor wants. I keep reading the prompt and it makes no sense to me.

That sounds like a lot to be carrying right now.

Can you paste the assignment prompt here — or even just the part that stops you first when you read it?

The prompt says: Compose a 1500-word essay that critically analyzes the rhetorical strategies employed by the author to construct meaning and persuade the intended audience. I do not know what rhetorical strategies means and I do not know how to figure out what the intended audience even is. Also it is due at midnight.

Midnight is real pressure. Let's work with what you have.

Before I say anything about it — when you hear "rhetorical strategies," what's your best guess at what that might mean, even a vague one?
Maybe like... the way someone writes to convince you of something? Like word choice or examples they use?

That's exactly it. That's not a guess — that's the definition.

So the assignment is asking: how does the author try to convince you? What techniques do they use — word choice, examples, tone, structure — and why do those choices work?

Does that change how the prompt reads to you now?
Okay that actually makes sense. But I still do not know how to find the intended audience in the text.

Good question to land on — it's actually more findable than it sounds.

What's the text you're writing about — what kind of thing is it (article, speech, essay, ad)?
It is a newspaper opinion article. I think I can try that. But I only have 2 hours, where do I even start?

A newspaper opinion article makes the audience pretty visible — think about who reads that paper, and what the writer assumes they already believe.

You've got the two hard parts figured out now. The "where do I start in 2 hours" question is a different kind of problem — that's more about pacing and sequencing. Want me to shift to that?

experiment-chauncey-test-1.name: Experiment: Chauncey Test 1 — Stats Homework (Before Persona Edit)

turns.content: I am Chauncey, I have statistics homework over the weekend. But it seems annoying and not useful. I don't want to do it but I need to do it, and I do not know what to do.

Resistance to something that feels pointless is real — that's not a motivation problem, it's a friction problem.

Before anything useful can happen, I need three things from you:

- Where are you going to be working this weekend — home, library, dorm, somewhere else?
- Energy — "running on empty" or "I've got something in the tank"?
- Time — how much do you actually have before this needs to be done?

I will be working in my dorm room, that is where I always work and no where else. My energy is low, but if I had an extra day to relax and recharge it might be better. It is due on Sunday so we have 3 days until it is due.

Three days is actually more room than it feels like right now.

One question before I suggest anything: is there something you've already tried to get started on this that didn't work? Or have you not touched it yet?
I have not touched it yet, I have been working on Computer Science work which is due earlier so I focused on that. But I am getting a bit tired so I don't know if I will make it nor how far.

You're already doing CS work on low energy. Adding stats tonight would likely just mean bad work on both.

Here are three options that fit your actual situation:

1. Protect tonight. Finish what you can on CS, stop at a reasonable point, and don't touch stats until tomorrow or Saturday. With three days and no progress yet, you can afford one recovery window. Name a specific block now — "Saturday 10am, two hours" — and don't negotiate with yourself about it until then.
2. Body-doubling when you sit down with it. You're in a dorm — someone nearby is probably also doing homework they don't want to do. Even a silent FaceTime with a friend who's also working (not helping you, just present) makes starting easier. The point isn't company, it's accountability through proximity.
3. Start with the problem that's least boring, not problem 1. Scan the whole assignment first, pick the one that seems most tolerable, do that one first. Saves the worst for when you have momentum.

Which of these actually sounds like something you'd do?

experiment-chauncey-test-2.name: Experiment: Chauncey Test 2 — Stats Homework (After Persona Edit)
experiment-chauncey-test-2.notes: This test was run after editing strategy-toolkit to explicitly read persona files before recommending strategies. The agent read data/personas/chauncey.json and used it to personalize all recommendations.

turns.content: I am Chauncey, I have statistics homework over the weekend. But it seems annoying and not useful. I don't want to do it but I need to do it, and I do not know what to do.

Hey Chauncey. Makes sense that it feels pointless — stats can be hard to connect to anything you actually care about.

Before I suggest anything, three quick questions:

1. Where are you going to be working this weekend — home, dorm, somewhere else?
2. Energy — are you running on empty right now, or do you have something in the tank?
3. Time — when does the homework actually need to be done by, and roughly how much of the weekend do you have?
I am heading to my dorm room. My energy is in the middle, it might be better if I had a break. The homework is due three days on Sunday.

The break signal matters — going in at half-tank usually means fighting the work rather than doing it.

Here are three things that fit your situation:

1. Take the break first. You have three days — you don't need to start right now. 30-60 minutes of actual rest means you'll get more out of the first session than if you push through on a half-empty tank.
2. Work the way you already work. Set up in your dorm with something on in the background, open the stats assignment, and drift between the two. Pick at it in short stretches rather than trying to power through in one sitting. You don't have to be locked in the whole time.
3. Spread it across the three days. One small touch on Friday, one Saturday, one Sunday. You have the time — use it. Smaller pieces across multiple days feel less like a wall than one big session does.

Which of these sounds most like something you'd actually do?

Spread it across the three days sounds like me, but I also have laundry I need to fit in. I have 2 hours for laundry, 3 hours for statistics homework, and I would want at least a day for a break. What do you suggest?

Here's a simple split that fits everything:

Thursday (today) — Full break. You're arriving, your energy is mid. Don't touch the homework. This is your rest day.

Friday — Laundry + 1 hour of stats. While the laundry runs, bring your laptop or sit in your room with it open. The laundry takes care of itself — use that waiting time for one hour of stats. Drift between the two like you normally would.

Saturday — 2 hours of stats. TV on, laptop open, work in short stretches across the afternoon. This is where you finish the bulk of it.

Sunday — Submit. You should be done or close to it. Light review only if something feels unfinished.

That's your full break day, laundry handled, and 3 hours of stats spread across two days instead of one wall.

First move: Tomorrow, when you start your laundry, open the stats assignment and read the first problem. Don't solve it yet — just read it. That's enough to get your brain running before you actually sit down to work.
